sentence: If I would know the answer, I would tell you.
correct: If I knew the answer, I would tell you.
explanation: Second conditional: <If> + past simple, not <would>. Use <If I knew>.

sentence: The cake was make by my grandmother.
correct: The cake was made by my grandmother.
explanation: Passive voice requires the past participle: <was made>, not <was make>.

sentence: If she will study harder, she will pass the exam.
correct: If she studies harder, she will pass the exam.
explanation: First conditional: <If> + present simple (not <will>). Use <If she studies>.

sentence: The letter was wrote by the manager.
correct: The letter was written by the manager.
explanation: Passive voice requires the past participle: <was written>, not <was wrote>.

sentence: If I had more time, I will learn to play the piano.
correct: If I had more time, I would learn to play the piano.
explanation: Second conditional: <If> + past simple, <would> + base verb. Not <will>.

sentence: The window was broke by the children.
correct: The window was broken by the children.
explanation: Passive voice requires the past participle: <was broken>, not <was broke>.

sentence: If it will rain tomorrow, we will stay at home.
correct: If it rains tomorrow, we will stay at home.
explanation: First conditional: <If> + present simple. Use <If it rains>, not <If it will rain>.

sentence: The homework has been did by all the students.
correct: The homework has been done by all the students.
explanation: Present perfect passive: <has been> + past participle. <Done>, not <did>.

sentence: If I was you, I would accept the offer.
correct: If I were you, I would accept the offer.
explanation: In second conditional with <I>, use <were> (subjunctive): <If I were you>.

sentence: The car is being repair at the garage.
correct: The car is being repaired at the garage.
explanation: Present continuous passive: <is being> + past participle. <Repaired>, not <repair>.

sentence: If they would arrive earlier, they wouldn't miss the train.
correct: If they arrived earlier, they wouldn't miss the train.
explanation: Second conditional: <If> + past simple. Use <If they arrived>, not <would arrive>.

sentence: English is spoke in many countries.
correct: English is spoken in many countries.
explanation: Passive voice: <is> + past participle. <Spoken>, not <spoke>.

sentence: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it will boil.
correct: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.
explanation: Zero conditional (facts): <If> + present simple, present simple. Not <will boil>.

sentence: The new hospital will be build next year.
correct: The new hospital will be built next year.
explanation: Future passive: <will be> + past participle. <Built>, not <build>.

sentence: If I would have studied harder, I would have passed.
correct: If I had studied harder, I would have passed.
explanation: Third conditional: <If> + past perfect. Use <If I had studied>, not <would have studied>.

sentence: The food was ate by the dog.
correct: The food was eaten by the dog.
explanation: Passive voice: <was> + past participle. <Eaten>, not <ate>.

sentence: If she would be taller, she could reach the shelf.
correct: If she were taller, she could reach the shelf.
explanation: Second conditional: <If> + past simple. Use <If she were>, not <would be>.

sentence: The report must be finish by Friday.
correct: The report must be finished by Friday.
explanation: Modal passive: <must be> + past participle. <Finished>, not <finish>.

sentence: If we don't hurry, we would miss the bus.
correct: If we don't hurry, we will miss the bus.
explanation: First conditional: <If> + present simple, <will> + base verb. Not <would>.

sentence: The books were stole from the library.
correct: The books were stolen from the library.
explanation: Passive voice: <were> + past participle. <Stolen>, not <stole>.

sentence: If I had known about the party, I would came.
correct: If I had known about the party, I would have come.
explanation: Third conditional: <would have> + past participle. <Would have come>, not <would came>.

sentence: The house was build in 1920.
correct: The house was built in 1920.
explanation: Passive voice: <was> + past participle. <Built>, not <build>.

sentence: If you mix blue and yellow, you will get green.
correct: If you mix blue and yellow, you get green.
explanation: Zero conditional (facts): both clauses use present simple. <You get>, not <will get>.

sentence: The song was sang by a famous singer.
correct: The song was sung by a famous singer.
explanation: Passive voice: <was> + past participle. <Sung>, not <sang>.

sentence: If she studied more, she will get better grades.
correct: If she studied more, she would get better grades.
If she studies more, she will get better grades.
explanation: Don't mix conditionals: either first (studies/will) or second (studied/would).
